topic: Primate skull morphology comparative analysis across MorphoSource collections

all_discoveries: Broad primate skull-related searches are productive on MorphoSource, with 'primate skull CT' returning 10 results and 'primate cranium mesh' returning 12.
Great ape-focused searches show clear signal, especially for Pan and Gorilla, each returning 10 results for skull-related queries.
Morphology-relevant modalities appear to include both CT-based volumetric data and mesh-derived cranial models, which is useful for comparative skull analysis.
Pongo is represented but appears less densely sampled in this initial pass, with only 2 results for 'Pongo skull CT'.
The search space is already separating into taxonomic and modality-based pathways: broad primate coverage versus ape genus-specific deepening.
Old World monkey-focused queries produced strong results: Macaca skull CT, Papio cranium mesh, and Cercopithecus skull scan each returned 10 results.
Strepsirrhine coverage also appears promising, with Eulemur skull CT returning 10 results.
More basal primate coverage is present as well, with Tarsius cranium scan returning 10 results.
The apparent success across catarrhines, strepsirrhines, and tarsiers suggests MorphoSource has a broadly distributed primate cranial dataset rather than one limited to great apes.
Both CT- and mesh-oriented terminology continue to retrieve material, reinforcing modality diversity for comparative skull morphology.

This iteration successfully filled a major taxonomic gap by showing strong MorphoSource search returns for several New World monkey cranial queries: Cebus, Saimiri, Alouatta, and Ateles all returned 10 results.
Macaca mulatta at the species level also returned 10 results, indicating that at least some productive genera can be searched below the genus level rather than only with broad taxonomic terms.
Across both previous and current iterations, MorphoSource appears to support broad primate cranial coverage spanning great apes, Old World monkeys, New World monkeys, strepsirrhines, and tarsiers.
Multiple modality/body-part phrasings continue to work well, including skull, cranium, CT, scan, and mesh, suggesting flexible search vocabulary for future exploration.

Species-level New World monkey queries remain high-yield: Cebus apella skull CT, Saimiri sciureus skull mesh, and Ateles geoffroyi skull CT each returned 10 results.
Old World monkey coverage also appears strong at species level, with Papio anubis skull mesh returning 12 results.
Lesser ape coverage is present beyond great apes, as Hylobates skull scan returned 10 results.
Across iterations, MorphoSource search signals now span major primate clades: great apes, cercopithecoids, New World monkeys, strepsirrhines, tarsiers, and gibbons.
Species-specific searching appears viable for at least some taxa, suggesting potential for more controlled comparative sampling rather than only genus-level aggregation.
Species-level great ape searches are high-yield: Pongo pygmaeus, Pongo abelii, and Pan troglodytes each returned 10 results for skull/cranium-focused queries.
Lesser apes remain well represented at species level, with Hylobates lar skull CT also returning 10 results.
Strepsirrhine coverage extends to species-level lemur material, with Eulemur fulvus skull mesh returning 10 results.
The search space now clearly spans major primate clades relevant to comparative skull morphology: great apes, lesser apes, cercopithecoids, platyrrhines, strepsirrhines, and tarsiers.
Both CT-oriented and mesh-oriented query formulations continue to succeed, suggesting multimodal cranial data may be available across taxa.

Species-level primate cranial queries continue to return strong hit counts across major clades, including Pan troglodytes, Pongo pygmaeus, Hylobates lar, Eulemur fulvus, and Colobus guereza.
The search pattern now covers apes, gibbons, lemurs, cercopithecoids, and multiple platyrrhine taxa, suggesting broad comparative potential for primate skull morphology within MorphoSource.
Pongo pygmaeus and Hylobates lar were replicated successfully with alternate anatomy/modality phrasing, increasing confidence that these are robust search pathways rather than one-off matches.
Colobus guereza adds a useful colobine/cercopithecoid branch that broadens Old World monkey representation beyond Macaca and Papio.
Uniformly high result counts indicate MorphoSource likely contains enough primate cranial media to support a multi-clade comparative sampling strategy.
This iteration adds major hominoid and strepsirrhine coverage with successful searches for Gorilla gorilla, Homo sapiens, Nomascus leucogenys, Trachypithecus, and Lemur catta.
Across current and prior iterations, MorphoSource now shows consistent primate cranial search returns spanning apes, Old World monkeys, New World monkeys, and lemuriform taxa.
All five new queries returned the platform cap of 10 results, reinforcing that cranial/skull-related primate materials are broadly represented rather than isolated to a few flagship taxa.
The dataset landscape appears especially promising for comparative analyses that include both great apes and non-ape primates, since repeated searches across those groups continue to succeed.
This iteration added strong coverage in Old World monkeys with Macaca mulatta and Papio anubis skull CT search hits.
The search space now includes additional strepsirrhine and nocturnal primate representation via Indri indri and Nycticebus skull CT results.
Tarsius skull CT results extend coverage toward tarsiers, improving representation outside the ape-monkey-lemur core already identified.
All five new primate skull CT queries returned 10 results, reinforcing the pattern that MorphoSource likely has broad primate cranial scan coverage across major lineages.

The cumulative query history now spans great apes, lesser apes, Old World monkeys, New World monkeys, lemurs, lorises, indriids, and tarsiers, suggesting strong potential for broad comparative skull morphology sampling.
All five new species/body-part targeted primate queries returned results, reinforcing that MorphoSource likely has broad cranial coverage across major primate clades.
Macaca mulatta and Papio anubis add stronger Old World monkey representation to the emerging comparative skull dataset landscape.
Tarsius results are especially valuable because they extend coverage into tarsiers, a key lineage for broad phylogenetic cranial comparison.
Indri indri and Nycticebus results suggest lemuriform and lorisiform cranial material is discoverable, improving strepsirrhine representation.
Using 'cranium CT' instead of prior 'skull CT' formulations still yielded hits, indicating query phrasing is flexible and search recall may be robust for cranial terms.

Broad primate skull-related searches continue to return results across all tested queries, reinforcing that MorphoSource likely contains substantial cranial material relevant to comparative morphology.
Eulemur-specific cranial CT results extend strepsirrhine coverage beyond previously found lemur taxa and suggest lemur cranial sampling may be richer than initially established.
Alouatta skull CT results add New World monkey representation, improving phylogenetic breadth for comparative skull analyses.

Generic searches such as 'microCT primate skull', 'full cranium primate CT', and 'mandible primate CT' all returned hits, indicating discoverability is not limited to species-name queries and that both whole-skull and lower-jaw material may be available.
The project now has positive search signals spanning apes, Old World monkeys, New World monkeys, tarsiers, lorises, and multiple lemuriform/strepsirrhine lineages.

Targeted species-level primate skull CT queries continued to return results across multiple major clades: strepsirrhines (Eulemur fulvus), New World monkeys (Alouatta palliata), Old World monkeys (Papio hamadryas), and hominoids (Pan troglodytes, Gorilla gorilla).
The cumulative search space now supports a broad comparative primate skull morphology program spanning deep phylogenetic divisions rather than a single lineage.
Species-specific searches appear to be as discoverable as broader genus-level or anatomy-term searches, suggesting MorphoSource indexing likely supports fine-grained taxonomic retrieval.
Hominoid cranial material seems especially promising because both chimpanzee and gorilla skull CT queries returned results, reinforcing earlier evidence from human-related searches.
Papionin and other cercopithecoid representation is expanding beyond Papio anubis to Papio hamadryas, indicating possible within-genus comparative opportunities.
Additional species-level skull CT searches succeeded across major primate clades: Macaca fascicularis, Pan paniscus, Gorilla beringei, Eulemur rubriventer, and Ateles geoffroyi.

The accumulated search history now covers strepsirrhines, platyrrhines, cercopithecoids, and hominoids with repeated positive retrieval, reinforcing broad taxonomic coverage for comparative primate cranial research on MorphoSource.
Hominoid coverage has deepened beyond genus-level confidence by adding bonobo and mountain gorilla alongside previously found chimpanzee and western gorilla searches.
Platyrrhine and strepsirrhine coverage also improved with Ateles geoffroyi and Eulemur rubriventer, suggesting comparative sampling is not limited to only commonly studied catarrhines.
Repeated species-level query success indicates that skull-focused CT terminology is a robust retrieval pattern on MorphoSource for primate taxa.

Species-level cranial CT searches continue to return results for additional primates, including Pan paniscus, Macaca fascicularis, and Ateles geoffroyi, reinforcing broad taxonomic coverage across apes, cercopithecoids, and platyrrhines.

Generic modality-based searches such as 'primate skull mesh' and 'primate skull microCT' also return results, suggesting that relevant cranial morphology data may exist in multiple digital formats beyond only CT-labeled records.

The consistency of positive hits across many prior and current primate queries supports the idea that MorphoSource is discoverable for comparative primate skull work at both broad and species-specific levels.
All five new genus-level primate cranial queries returned results, reinforcing that MorphoSource has broad skull-related coverage across apes, cercopithecoids, atelines, papionins, and lemurs.
Search success extends beyond CT phrasing to additional modality wording such as 'mesh', 'microCT', and generic 'scan', suggesting multimodal discoverability for primate skull material.
The new hits for Pan, Macaca, Ateles, Papio, and Eulemur align with previously successful species-level searches, indicating that both genus-level and species-level query formulations are productive.
Papio and Eulemur remain especially useful lineage anchors because they connect earlier CT-style queries with new modality variants, implying stable retrieval across terminology changes.
MorphoSource appears strong for comparative primate cranial prospecting, but the evidence is still at the search-surface level rather than the specimen-audit level.
All five new taxon-targeted queries succeeded, reinforcing that MorphoSource has broad discoverability for primate cranial material.
The newly searched taxa extend coverage across major primate clades: gibbons (Hylobates), Old World monkeys (Colobus), strepsirrhines (Galago, Nycticebus), and tarsiers (Tarsius).
The repeated success of 'skull CT' searches across both previously tried and newly tried genera suggests that cranial CT-related metadata are consistently indexed on MorphoSource.

The project now has evidence of searchable cranial data spanning apes, cercopithecoids, platyrrhines, lemurs, lorises, galagos, tarsiers, and gibbons, which is promising for broad comparative skull morphology studies.

next_directions: Audit the top results for 3-5 representative taxa across clades (for example Pan, Macaca, Hylobates, Tarsius, Nycticebus) and record specimen completeness, media type, access status, and institution.
Search by institution names discovered in audited records to identify concentrated primate skull holdings and collection-level strengths.
Test anatomical specificity with queries such as 'cranium', 'mandible', 'skull roof', or species-level combinations to separate full skull datasets from mixed cranial elements.
Compare modality labels within the same taxon (CT, microCT, mesh, surface model) to determine which forms are most common and most suitable for morphometric analysis.
Look for intraspecific replication within a few focal taxa to assess whether MorphoSource supports statistical comparative studies rather than isolated exemplar specimens.
final_memory.summary:
This iteration strengthens the case that MorphoSource is a fertile resource for primate skull morphology because every new search returned results, and the new genera expand coverage into additional major primate lineages. The cumulative search history now spans hominoids, Old World monkeys, New World monkeys, lemurs, lorises, galagos, tarsiers, and gibbons, suggesting that searchable cranial CT-related media are not confined to a few charismatic taxa but are broadly distributed across the primate tree.

However, the strategic gain is still limited by the same structural problem seen in prior rounds: query-level success does not translate into knowledge about dataset quality. Because all searches again cap at 10 results, the agent cannot infer relative abundance, and without opening records it still cannot tell whether the results are full skull scans, partial cranial elements, derivative meshes, or inaccessible media. The research should now pivot from taxonomic expansion to specimen- and collection-level auditing so the next iterations can identify which MorphoSource collections are genuinely usable for comparative cranial morphometrics.
